title: C# Advanced — Paper 2
description: Advanced C# covering Generic Variance, LINQ Core & Advanced Operators, Lambda & Functional Patterns, Delegates/Func/Action/Predicate, Memory Management, Span<T>/Memory<T>, Reflection, Attributes, and Extension Methods.

questions.text: What is the difference between a delegate and an event in C#?

options.text: A delegate can be invoked from outside the declaring class; an event restricts invocation to within the declaring class.
An event can hold multiple delegates; a delegate can only hold one.
Delegates are only used for async operations.
Events cannot be multicast; delegates can.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly declares a Func<> delegate that takes two ints and returns a bool?

options.text: Func<int, bool, int> f;
Func<int, int, bool> f;
Action<int, int, bool> f;
Predicate<int, int> f;

questions.text: What does the LINQ Join operator produce?

options.text: A cross product of all elements from both sequences
An inner join: matches elements from two sequences based on a key selector, returning a flat result
A left outer join that preserves all elements of the first sequence
A union of both sequences without duplicates

questions.text: What does LINQ's GroupJoin return that distinguishes it from a regular Join?

options.text: It returns only matching pairs like an inner join.
It returns each element from the outer sequence paired with a (possibly empty) collection of matching inner elements, enabling left outer join semantics.
It groups elements of a single sequence by a key.
It joins sequences and removes duplicates using a comparer.

questions.text: What is a higher-order function in C#?

options.text: A function defined in a higher-priority assembly
A function that takes one or more functions as parameters or returns a function as its result
A function that runs on a higher-priority thread
A static method defined on a base class

questions.text: In C#, how is method resolution performed when a generic type has an interface constraint?

options.text: The compiler boxes the value and calls via dynamic dispatch.
The JIT generates specialized code for each type argument and can call interface methods directly via the constraint, avoiding boxing for structs.
Generic constraints are checked only at runtime, not compile time.
The constraint forces all calls to go through reflection.

questions.text: In C# covariance, which of the following is a valid assignment?

options.text: IEnumerable<object> list = new List<int>();
IEnumerable<object> list = new List<string>();
List<object> list = new List<string>();
IList<object> list = new List<string>();

questions.text: What is the difference between Select and SelectMany in LINQ?

options.text: Select flattens nested sequences; SelectMany projects each element to a single value.
Select projects each element to a new form; SelectMany projects each element to a sequence and flattens all results into one sequence.
SelectMany returns a dictionary; Select returns a list.
They are interchangeable.

questions.text: Which LINQ methods force immediate execution rather than deferred execution?

options.text: Where, Select, OrderBy
ToList, ToArray, Count, First
GroupBy, SelectMany, Zip
Take, Skip, Distinct

questions.text: What does currying mean in the context of C# functional programming?

options.text: Applying all arguments to a function at once
Transforming a function that takes multiple arguments into a chain of functions that each take a single argument
Caching the result of a pure function
Converting a lambda into a delegate type

questions.text: What is the purpose of the 'where' clause filter (exception filter) introduced in C# 6?

options.text: It filters exceptions before they unwind the stack, allowing a catch block to run only if the filter condition is true, without unwinding if false.
It is a LINQ where clause used inside a catch block.
It lets you catch multiple exception types in one catch block.
It re-routes exceptions to a different handler.

questions.text: What happens if Dispose() throws an exception while the 'using' statement is handling another exception?

options.text: Both exceptions are combined into an AggregateException.
The exception from Dispose() replaces the original exception, and the original exception is lost.
The Dispose() exception is swallowed silently.
The CLR logs both exceptions and rethrows the original.

questions.text: What is the unsafe keyword required for when working with Span<T> and raw pointers?

options.text: It allows async methods inside a Span<T> context.
It enables pointer arithmetic and direct memory manipulation, bypassing the CLR's safety guarantees, required for pointer types and stackalloc in pointer contexts.
It marks Span<T> allocations for the large object heap.
It disables JIT optimizations for a method.

questions.text: What does MethodInfo.Invoke() return when calling a void method via reflection?

options.text: It returns 0 as an integer.
It returns null.
It throws a VoidMethodException.
It returns Type.Missing.

questions.text: Which attribute targets should you specify in AttributeUsage to allow a custom attribute on both classes and methods?

options.text: [AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.All)]
[AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.Class | AttributeTargets.Method)]
[AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.Class, AttributeTargets.Method)]
[AttributeUsage(AttributeTargets.ClassOrMethod)]

questions.text: What is an extension method in C#?

options.text: A method defined inside the class it extends.
A static method in a static class with 'this' as the first parameter modifier, allowing it to be called as if it were an instance method on the extended type.
A method that overrides a virtual method in a base class.
A method added to a class via reflection at runtime.

questions.text: Which generic interface would you implement to define a type-safe comparer that avoids boxing for value types?

options.text: IComparable
IComparer<T>
IEquatable
IComparableBox<T>

questions.text: What does the 'in' variance keyword on a generic type parameter indicate?

options.text: The type parameter can only appear in output positions (return types).
The type parameter is contravariant: it can only appear in input positions, allowing assignment from a more general type to a more specific one.
The type parameter is covariant.
It makes the type parameter read-only inside the generic class.

questions.text: What does LINQ's OrderBy return, and how do you chain a secondary sort?

options.text: It returns IEnumerable<T>; secondary sort uses OrderBy again.
It returns IOrderedEnumerable<T>; you chain ThenBy or ThenByDescending for secondary sorts.
It returns IQueryable<T>; secondary sort uses .Sort().
It returns a sorted List<T>; secondary sort requires re-calling OrderBy.

questions.text: What does LINQ's Zip operator do?

options.text: It compresses a sequence for serialization.
It combines two sequences element-by-element using a result selector, stopping when the shorter sequence is exhausted.
It concatenates two sequences end-to-end.
It groups elements from two sequences by a common key.

questions.text: How does a multicast delegate work in C#?

options.text: It can only target methods with matching parameter types, unlike regular delegates.
It holds an invocation list of multiple method references; when invoked, it calls all methods in order, and the return value is the last method's return value.
It invokes all methods simultaneously on separate threads.
It merges return values from all methods using LINQ.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly creates a Predicate<T> that checks if a string is longer than 5 characters?

options.text: Predicate<string> p = s => s.Length > 5;
Predicate<string, bool> p = s => s.Length > 5;
Func<string, string> p = s => s.Length > 5;
Action<string> p = s => s.Length > 5;

questions.text: What is the purpose of using a WeakReference in C# to avoid memory leaks with events?

options.text: It makes events thread-safe automatically.
It allows the event handler subscriber to be garbage collected even if still subscribed, because the event only holds a weak reference to the handler's target object.
It compresses the event handler in memory.
It prevents duplicate subscriptions to an event.

questions.text: What is the GC generation model in .NET and how does it affect object lifetime?

options.text: There is only one GC generation; all objects are collected at the same time.
The GC has generations 0, 1, and 2. Short-lived objects start in gen 0 and are collected frequently; surviving objects promote to higher generations which are collected less often.
Generations are based on object size, not lifetime.
The GC generations only apply to value types on the stack.

questions.text: How can you use reflection to get the value of a property named 'Name' from an object instance?

options.text: obj.GetProperty("Name").Value
obj.GetType().GetProperty("Name").GetValue(obj)
PropertyInfo.Get(obj, "Name")
typeof(obj).Name

questions.text: When AllowMultiple = true is set in AttributeUsage, what does it enable?

options.text: The attribute can target multiple element types.
The same attribute can be applied more than once to the same code element.
The attribute can be inherited by derived classes.
The attribute can be applied to both methods and properties.

questions.text: In a fluent builder pattern using extension methods, what does each fluent method typically return?

options.text: void, modifying state internally
The same type (or the builder type) to allow method chaining
A Task to enable async chaining
A boolean indicating success

questions.text: What restriction applies to ref structs like Span<T>?

options.text: They cannot have generic type parameters.
They cannot be stored on the heap: they cannot be boxed, used as generic type arguments, assigned to object/interface variables, or captured in lambdas/async methods.
They can only contain value types.
They cannot implement interfaces.

questions.text: Which LINQ method computes a running total across a sequence and is equivalent to SQL's SUM over a set?

options.text: Aggregate
Sum
Reduce
Accumulate

questions.text: What is the correct way to define an extension method for IEnumerable<T> in a static class?

options.text: public IEnumerable<T> MyExt<T>(IEnumerable<T> source) { }
public static IEnumerable<T> MyExt<T>(this IEnumerable<T> source) { }
public static IEnumerable<T> MyExt<T>(extend IEnumerable<T> source) { }
extension IEnumerable<T> { IEnumerable<T> MyExt<T>() { } }
